title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: To generate datasets necessary to train a machine learning model to link protein sequence to function (PAM specificity), we sought to engineer and characterize a sufficiently large collection of novel SpCas9 enzymes with altered PAM preferences.
context: using a structure–function-informed approach based on SpCas9 structures (Ref: 10) and previous engineering efforts (Ref: 3,14,42,43)... These six amino acids modulate specificity for the 3rd and 4th PAM bases in the context of the engineered enzymes SpCas9-VRER, SpCas9-VRQR and SpG (Ref: 3,14,42) (Supplementary Note 3).
outcome: We generated a saturation mutagenesis library of SpCas9 variant enzymes... simultaneously mutating six amino acid residues within the SpCas9 PI domain, resulting in a library with theoretical complexity of 64 million enzymes (the SpCas9(6AA) library) (Fig. 1c, Extended Data Fig. 2a and Supplementary Note 3). We selected residues D1135, S1136, G1218, E1219, R1335 and T1337 for saturation mutagenesis, and individual enzymes within the library are hereafter named according to amino acid identities at these six positions.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: Expansion of our library to also mutate R1333, the residue that contacts the second guanine of the SpCas9 PAM (Ref: 10), largely abrogated enzyme activity (Supplementary Note 3).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: To identify SpCas9 PAM variant enzymes with activity on non-canonical PAMs
context: We therefore focused our efforts on altering the 3rd and 4th positions of the PAM.
outcome: we performed an extensive set of bacterial-based positive selection assays (Ref: 14,44) to select for enzymes that are capable of cleaving target sites bearing each of the 16 possible NGNN PAMs, which have a fixed G nucleotide at the second position and that vary at the third and fourth positions (Extended Data Fig. 2b,c and Supplementary Note 4).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: Across the 16 experiments using the SpCas9(6AA) library, we obtained 634 unique SpCas9 enzymes that survived the selections (Supplementary Table 1). We observed PAM-dependent enrichment of amino acids across the enzymes depending on the 3rd or 4th nucleotide of the PAM used for the selection (Extended Data Fig. 2c).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: Next, we linked amino acid sequence to PAM specificity for the 634 recovered SpCas9 enzymes.
outcome: To identify the amino acid substitutions in the SpCas9 enzymes, we performed an arrayed plasmid sequencing method for rapid and inexpensive whole open reading frame identification based on multiplex PCR (Ref: 45) (Supplementary Fig. 1). The PAM specificities of each of the 634 SpCas9 enzymes were then characterized using a high-throughput PAM determination assay (Ref: 46) (HT-PAMDA; Fig. 1c,d, Supplementary Table 1 and Supplementary Fig. 2). HT-PAMDA comprehensively measures rate constants (k) of nuclease cleavage for an enzyme across a library of substrates encoding all possible PAMs, providing kinetic data to quantify the global PAM profile of an enzyme.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: HT-PAMDA experiments revealed that the bacterial selections performed using the SpCas9(6AA) library identified enzymes capable of recognizing each of the 16 NGNN PAMs (Fig. 1e and Supplementary Fig. 2). However, few enzymes were most active on the specific PAM that they were selected on (Supplementary Figs. 2 and 3)
outcome: highlighting a limitation of the bacterial-based selection approach.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: Clustering the enzymes by their HT-PAMDA-determined PAM profiles revealed eight main classes of PAM variant enzymes that were each associated with specific amino acid substitutions (Fig. 1d). The two most common classes of SpCas9 enzymes represented in our dataset from the SpCas9(6AA) library selections were those with relaxed NGN PAM (similar to SpG or SpCas9-NG) or NGG PAM (similar to wild-type SpCas9) but with up to four amino acid substitutions compared with previously described enzymes (Supplementary Figs. 4 and 5). Most interestingly, our bacterial-based selections also revealed various enzymes with preferences for non-canonical nucleotides in the 3rd and 4th positions of the PAM. In many cases, we observed a nucleotide preference at the 4th nucleotide of the PAM, extending the PAM (specifying three bases instead of two).
context: as has been demonstrated for other previously described enzymes (Ref: 14).
outcome: A preference for an extended PAM may hold advantages for minimizing off-targets... Together, these observations highlight the previously uncharacterized plasticity of the SpCas9 PI domain.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: We therefore sought to rationally design more optimal PAM-selective enzymes
context: Although our bacterial selection experiments led to the evolution of SpCas9 enzymes with novel PAM requirements, the most efficiently targeted PAM of an enzyme did not always correlate with the PAM on which that enzyme was selected (Fig. 1e and Supplementary Figs. 2 and 3).
outcome: by testing 'consensus' enzymes for each of the 16 NGNN PAMs, assessed by combining the most enriched amino acids at each of the six positions recovered from each selection (Extended Data Fig. 2c).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: When assessing the PAM requirements of the consensus enzymes using HT-PAMDA (Supplementary Fig. 6a), most enzymes did not efficiently target the PAM for which they were designed, with only 4 of 31 consensus enzymes exhibiting maximal efficiency against their intended PAMs (Fig. 1e and Supplementary Fig. 6b). The rationally designed consensus enzymes generally had weaker efficiencies than the enzymes obtained directly from bacterial selections (Fig. 1e and Supplementary Fig. 6a).
outcome: These results indicate that mutations at these six positions do not contribute to PAM preference independently, but rather interact with one another in an epistatic manner; selections and rational design alone are thus insufficient to systematically obtain enzymes with desired PAMs.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: To approximate the fraction of the 64 million enzymes encoded with our SpCas9(6AA) library that retained editing activity on any PAM
outcome: we performed HT-PAMDA on 135 randomly chosen library members.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: Unexpectedly, we found that approximately 18% of the enzymes in the SpCas9(6AA) library were capable of editing on at least one PAM, many of them with non-canonical PAM preferences (Fig. 1f).
outcome: This observation suggested that among the millions of possible combinations of residues in our six-position library, more than 10 million enzymes may be functional, the vast majority of which remain uncharacterized.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Learning PAM preference from amino acid sequence
main_content: We therefore sought to explore the entire fitness landscape of our SpCas9(6AA) library via machine learning.
context: As we could experimentally characterize only a small number of enzymes from the SpCas9(6AA) library (approximately 0.001%), and our data suggest that a substantial fraction of the enzymes may be active on at least one PAM (more than 15%; Fig. 1f), we envisioned that many additional enzymes with useful PAM requirements remained to be characterized. This, together with the observation that enriched amino acid substitutions were often neither necessary nor sufficient to predict the PAM preference of most enzymes, led us to seek a more systematic method to investigate the relationship between amino acid sequence and PAM specificity.
outcome: We utilized our two HT-PAMDA datasets as training data for a machine learning model, including functional enzymes derived from our bacterial selections on the 16 NGNN PAMs and random (non-selected) enzymes from the SpCas9(6AA) library. We reasoned that including some inactive enzymes in our training set would provide information about combinations of amino acids that lead to non-functional enzymes. To account for the imbalanced nature of our training data towards NGG-targeting enzymes and relaxed variants that are most active on NGTG PAMs (Fig. 1f), we assigned a label to each training example based on its most active PAM and randomly oversampled to balance across PAM classes. We trained machine learning models to predict the rate constant k on each of the 64 PAMs (positions 2–4) when provided with a 6AA sequence as an input (Fig. 2a), and compared the ability of different machine learning models to extrapolate to amino acid combinations not encountered in the training set. Linear regression, random forest and neural network models were tested in combination with different feature encodings as inputs to the model (one-hot encoding of each amino acid substitution; one-hot encoding of all single plus pairwise amino acid combinations; and Georgiev (Ref: 47), a physiochemical descriptor that can improve performance of some machine learning models for protein engineering (Ref: 25); Extended Data Fig. 3a). Model architectures and amino acid encoding were compared using an internal fivefold cross-validation (Extended Data Fig. 3a).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Learning PAM preference from amino acid sequence
main_content: The use of pairwise amino acid features was necessary for the linear and random forest, but not neural network, models to achieve generalizability to enzymes dissimilar from the training set (Hamming distance from training set of 4). The neural network model with one-hot encoding was the most generalizable while requiring the simplest input feature set
outcome: thus this model was named PAMmla and was used for subsequent analyses.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Learning PAM preference from amino acid sequence
main_content: We evaluated PAMmla on a test set comprising a random 20% of enzymes from our HT-PAMDA dataset that were held out from training, revealing accurate k predictions for unseen SpCas9 enzymes (Pearson’s r = 0.91; Fig. 2b). We also evaluated PAMmla using two additional random train test splits that showed similar correlations (both r = 0.92; Extended Data Fig. 3b)
outcome: demonstrating that these results are consistent regardless of the allocation of enzymes into training and testing sets.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Learning PAM preference from amino acid sequence
main_content: The test set was further subdivided into progressively more challenging subsets containing enzymes with increasing numbers of mutations relative to the most similar enzyme in the training set
outcome: demonstrating generalizability to enzymes that are dissimilar to those on which the model was trained (Fig. 2c).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Learning PAM preference from amino acid sequence
main_content: Across different PAM classes, the model performed relatively consistently
outcome: indicating the ability to generate accurate predictions, including for PAM classes that had relatively few training examples (Extended Data Figs. 3c–f).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Learning PAM preference from amino acid sequence
main_content: PAMmla models trained with oversampling to balance PAM classes within the training set improved performance on under-represented PAM classes in the test set (Extended Data Fig. 3g).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Learning PAM preference from amino acid sequence
main_content: In addition to accurately predicting PAM preferences of active SpCas9 enzymes, PAMmla was also capable of distinguishing active from inactive enzymes with high accuracy (area under the receiver operating curve (AUROC) = 0.99; Fig. 2d). With 'inactive' enzymes defined using a cut-off of maximum rate constant of less than 10−4.3, PAMmla correctly classified enzymes as active versus inactive for 149 of 154 enzymes (97%) in the test set (Fig. 2e).
context: We selected k less than 10−4.3 to define a non-targetable PAM since the HT-PAMDA k values for bona fide inactive enzymes are noisy between ks of 10−5 to 10−4.3 (Extended Data Figs. 3h,i and Supplementary Note 5).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Learning PAM preference from amino acid sequence
main_content: We tested the ability of PAMmla to recapitulate HT-PAMDA-determined PAM profiles of preexisting rationally designed or evolved SpCas9 PAM variant enzymes not included in the training or test sets. We were able to generate accurate PAM profiles for SpG, VRER and VRQR (Pearson’s r = 0.99, 0.71 and 0.93, respectively), each of which encodes two mutations relative to the closest member of the training set (Fig. 2f–h). We also predicted the PAM profile of xCas9, an enzyme that contains only a single PI domain mutation, E1219V, but additional mutations outside of the PI domain (Ref: 2). When predicting the impact of the single E1219V mutation, PAMmla produced a predicted PAM profile remarkably similar to the experimental data for xCas9 (Fig. 2i)
outcome: supporting previous evidence that the single E1219V mutation was the major contributor to the altered PAM of xCas9 (Ref: 3).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Machine learning-assisted prediction of PAM variant enzymes
main_content: We then utilized PAMmla to discover novel PAM variant enzymes
outcome: by predicting the PAM profiles for each of the 64 million possible enzymes in the SpCas9(6AA) library (Supplementary Fig. 7). The resulting enzymes were sorted by their predicted PAM profiles on activity alone (kPAM) or selectivity (kPAM/sum(kall_PAMs)) for each of the 16 NGNN PAMs (Fig. 3a). Among the top predicted enzymes for each criterion and PAM, we chose between 3 and 10 enzymes to experimentally validate PAMmla predictions using HT-PAMDA (for 281 enzymes; Fig. 3b, Supplementary Table 2 and Supplementary Fig. 8a–c).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Machine learning-assisted prediction of PAM variant enzymes
main_content: Among this group of enzymes, the PAMmla predictions for each PAM correlated with experimentally obtained ks (Pearson’s r = 0.90; Fig. 3c and Supplementary Figs. 8a–c and 9).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Machine learning-assisted prediction of PAM variant enzymes
main_content: Most of the top predicted enzymes encoded two or three mutations (and some up to four) relative to the most similar previously characterized variant (Fig. 3d)
outcome: indicating that PAMmla can accurately predict the PAM requirements of enzymes dissimilar from the training set.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Machine learning-assisted prediction of PAM variant enzymes
main_content: Clustering the experimentally determined PAM profiles from the PAMmla-predicted enzymes revealed ten main clusters (Fig. 3b,e). Clusters comprised new enzymes with novel PAM requirements compared with those previously seen (for example, NGTC, NGCT, NGCM (M = A or C) and relaxed with NGG anti-preference), along with sequence-diversified examples of enzymes with PAM profiles similar to those obtained from bacterial selections (for example, NGG, NGN and NGC).
outcome: These classes of enzymes provide new insight into combinations of amino acids within the SpCas9(6AA) library that enable useful PAM profiles.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Machine learning-assisted prediction of PAM variant enzymes
main_content: Sorting PAMmla-predicted enzymes for maximized efficiency on each of the 16 NGNN PAMs resulted in enzymes with higher k values on the PAM of interest than those obtained from bacterial selections (Fig. 3f). Although highly efficient enzymes with activity on a PAM of interest with k > 10−2.5 (similar to the maximum k of SpG) were rarely recovered from bacterial selections (less than 9% of enzymes), the majority of PAMmla-derived enzymes (56%) designed to maximize activity had k > 10−2.5 on the PAM for which they were sorted and had maximal efficiency on that PAM (Fig. 3f and Supplementary Fig. 9).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Machine learning-assisted prediction of PAM variant enzymes
main_content: Furthermore, when we sorted PAMmla predictions for selectivity on a PAM of interest, the resulting enzymes were more specific for the target PAM than enzymes derived from bacterial selections (Fig. 3g). Approximately 60% of PAMmla enzymes designed to maximize selectivity had a maximal experimentally determined k on the specific 4-nt PAM for which sorting was performed (with 91% of PAMmla enzymes having the intended PAM in the top four; Fig. 3g and Supplementary Fig. 9). By contrast, only 26% of enzymes from bacterial selections exhibited maximal activity on the PAM from which they were selected (Fig. 3g and Supplementary Fig. 3). When ranked among PAMmla predictions of 64 million enzymes, bacterial selection-derived enzymes ranked on average in the 95th percentile in terms of activity (outranked by 3.2 million enzymes) and the 70th percentile in terms of selectivity (outranked by 18.9 million enzymes; Supplementary Fig. 10a,b)
outcome: highlighting the utility of PAMmla to predict superior enzymes versus those derived from experimental selections. Thus, PAMmla predictions can be used to obtain enzymes tailored to the PAM of interest rather than generalist enzymes with relaxed PAMs, which tend to be the most common outcome of experimental engineering methods.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Machine learning-assisted prediction of PAM variant enzymes
main_content: We investigated potential functional roles of the amino acid substitutions in PAMmla-predicted enzymes (Supplementary Note 6).
outcome: Using shapely additive explanations (SHAP) (Ref: 48) analysis of machine learning feature importance (Extended Data Fig. 4) and structural modelling (Extended Data Fig. 5)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Machine learning-assisted prediction of PAM variant enzymes
main_content: we observed that mutations in PAMmla enzymes can be divided into three categories that either: (1) alter 3rd PAM position preference (Extended Data Fig. 5a–c); (2) alter 4th PAM position preference (Extended Data Fig. 5d–f); or (3) modify PAM recognition via base-independent interactions (Extended Data Fig. 5g–i). Generally, more selective PAMmla enzymes harboured mutations that form base-specific contacts with the DNA bases of the PAM, whereas PAMmla enzymes with broader PAM specificity shared nonspecific activity-potentiating mutations with previously described PAM-relaxed enzymes that form nonspecific contacts with the DNA backbone (Ref: 3,6). Many PAMmla enzymes shared combinations of mutations from both categories
outcome: potentially achieving a balance between specifying novel PAMs and retaining high activity.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Testing PAMmla-predicted enzymes in human cells
main_content: We tested the editing efficiencies of 22 PAMmla-generated enzymes in HEK 293T cells... We also assessed 14 enzymes from the training set that were identified from our bacterial selection experiments.
context: prioritizing those with preferences for PAMs that have not been previously described in the literature: NGAT, NGCM (M = A or C), NGCN, NGTN, NGTC, NGDC (D = A, G or T) and NGTG (Extended Data Fig. 6a and Supplementary Fig. 8a–c).
outcome: Nuclease forms of these 36 enzymes were assessed for generating insertion or deletion mutations (indels) across 32 endogenous target sites in HEK 293T cells compared with SpG, which is an engineered SpCas9 nuclease enzyme variant capable of targeting sites with NGN PAMs (Ref: 3).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Testing PAMmla-predicted enzymes in human cells
main_content: For both enzyme classes, predicted PAM requirements correlated well with their HT-PAMDA-determined profiles (Supplementary Fig. 11).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Testing PAMmla-predicted enzymes in human cells
main_content: In general, PAMmla enzymes exhibited similar or improved average editing efficiencies compared with SpG (Fig. 4a and Extended Data Fig. 6b). Although we only highlight a single enzyme from each PAM class (Fig. 4a), for each target site that we assessed, several PAMmla enzymes often achieved similar or greater editing efficiencies than SpG while maintaining a more selective PAM preference (Extended Data Fig. 6b and Supplementary Fig. 12a–g). In some cases, PAMmla enzymes were substantially more efficient at creating indels than the most similar variants from the training set. For instance, the PAMmla enzyme LWKYQS was capable of approximately 1.5-fold higher average editing efficiencies on sites with NGCM PAMs than its nearest neighbour from the training set, LWKYSS, which contains only one amino acid difference (Extended Data Fig. 6b, NGCM variants).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Testing PAMmla-predicted enzymes in human cells
main_content: Editing efficiencies varied by target site (Supplementary Fig. 12a–g)
outcome: suggesting spacer-specific differences and thus the potential utility of testing multiple PAMmla enzymes for each target of interest.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Testing PAMmla-predicted enzymes in human cells
main_content: to assess remaining activity against the ancestral PAM (Fig. 4b and Supplementary Fig. 12h).
outcome: We also tested all PAMmla enzymes on a control site bearing an NGG PAM

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Testing PAMmla-predicted enzymes in human cells
main_content: PAMmla enzymes predicted to poorly target NGG exhibited greatly diminished activity on the control NGG PAM target site (Supplementary Fig. 12i)
outcome: indicating that PAMmla can generate enzymes that minimize editing on the canonical PAM. Together, these observations demonstrate that PAMmla-predicted enzymes improve human cell-based editing efficiencies compared with SpG or those derived from bacterial selections, and that PAMmla can be used to generate enzymes with altered rather than relaxed PAM specificities.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Testing PAMmla-predicted enzymes in human cells
main_content: to assess whether they could be beneficial for A-to-G and C-to-T editing in the contexts of ABE8e (Ref: 50) and TadCBEd (Ref: 51) architectures, respectively.
context: PAM variants are useful in the context of base editing since precise positioning of Cas9 enzymes over the target base is required (Ref: 49).
outcome: For each of the seven PAM categories tested above, we therefore selected a single PAMmla-derived enzyme

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Testing PAMmla-predicted enzymes in human cells
main_content: For ABE8e and TadCBEd enzymes, we observed efficient base editing in HEK 293T cells at three genomic loci harbouring the preferred PAM of each of the corresponding Cas9 variants (Fig. 4c,d, Extended Data Fig. 6c,d and Supplementary Figs. 13 and 14). The base-editing efficiencies of PAMmla-generated base editors were superior to the PAM-relaxed SpRY in both ABE8e and TadCBEd contexts. PAMmla enzymes were more active versus SpG as ABE8e constructs (Fig. 4c) and similarly efficient as TadCBEd constructs depending on the site (Fig. 4d).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Testing PAMmla-predicted enzymes in human cells
main_content: We additionally tested PAMmla enzymes on a therapeutic base edit for sickle cell disease, achieving comparable on-target editing and fewer bystander edits compared with a previously reported approach (Ref: 52) (Supplementary Fig. 15a–h and Supplementary Note 7).
outcome: Together, these observations demonstrate that PAMmla-derived editors are generally more efficient as nucleases and base editors compared with PAM relaxed enzymes.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Specificity of PAMmla-predicted enzymes
main_content: PAMmla-predicted enzymes with altered PAM requirements should encounter fewer potential off-targets due to their more limited targeting ranges.
context: SpCas9 enzymes with relaxed PAM requirements are more prone to off-target editing as they search a larger fraction of the genome and thus encounter a larger number of potential off-target sites (Ref: 3,6,21).
outcome: To test this hypothesis, we utilized a cell-based unbiased genome-wide off-target assay, GUIDE-seq2 (Ref: 53), to identify nuclease-cleaved off-target sites.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Specificity of PAMmla-predicted enzymes
main_content: We observed comparable or greater on-target editing using PAMmla enzymes compared with SpG and SpRY at the sites chosen for GUIDE-seq2 (Fig. 4e and Extended Data Fig. 7a), and yet the total number of off-target sites were reduced with PAMmla enzymes versus SpG and SpRY (ranging from 26% to 93% reduction compared with SpG, and 49% to 96% reduction compared with SpRY; Fig. 4f). PAMmla enzymes resulted in a higher proportion of on-to-off-target GUIDE-seq2 reads relative to SpG and SpRY for all sites, including those with modest (Fig. 4g) and high (Fig. 4h) numbers of off-target sites. Off-target sites detected for each PAMmla-predicted enzyme were largely also detected in the SpG or SpRY sample with the same single guide RNA (sgRNA), although some off-targets were specific to the altered PAM variant enzymes (Extended Data Fig. 7b and Supplementary Note 8). The PAMs observed at off-target sites for each PAMmla-derived enzyme reflected their cognate HT-PAMDA-determined PAMs, whereas the aggregate PAMs observed for SpG and SpRY were more relaxed (Extended Data Fig. 7c).
outcome: These results demonstrate that PAMmla enzymes with altered PAMs can reduce genome-wide off-targets compared with PAM-relaxed enzymes.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Specificity of PAMmla-predicted enzymes
main_content: To verify the on-target and off-target benefits of PAMmla versus generalist enzymes in human patient-derived cells, we sought to correct a CYBB mutation in cells from an individual with X-linked chronic granulomatous disease (X-CGD).
context: Patients with X-CGD are highly susceptible to invasive infections and hyperinflammation that results in substantial morbidity and early mortality. CYBB mutations may be correctable through ex vivo editing of haematopoietic stem cells (Ref: 54,55).
outcome: Using patient-derived Epstein–Barr virus-transformed B cells harbouring the CYBBT362I mutation, we identified a target site with an NGAT PAM that positions the causative G-to-A mutation at position 8 within the protospacer (Fig. 4i) and electroporated mRNA encoding either ABE8e-SpG or the PAMmla-derived ABE8e-KWRQLC enzyme along with the A8 sgRNA.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Specificity of PAMmla-predicted enzymes
main_content: Both A-to-G base editors (ABEs) resulted in highly efficient levels of T362I correction (up to more than 90%) with minimal bystander editing (Fig. 4j).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: Specificity of PAMmla-predicted enzymes
main_content: GUIDE-seq2 assessment of off-targets using the CYBBT362I-targeted sgRNA in HEK 293T cells revealed only a single low-likelihood off-target site detected when using the PAMmla-predicted KWRQLC enzyme compared with five off-target sites with SpG (Extended Data Fig. 7e)
outcome: resulting in a superior on-to-off-target ratio for the PAMmla enzyme compared with the PAM-relaxed SpG (Fig. 4k).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: to predict SpCas9 enzymes for allele-specific editing (Ref: 56) by maximizing activity on a PAM of interest while minimizing activity on another.
context: One potential application of PAMmla is to predict SpCas9 enzymes for allele-specific editing (Ref: 56)... Sorting the PAMmla predictions for all 64 million SpCas9(6AA) enzymes is cumbersome
outcome: so we developed an in silico-directed evolution (ISDE) approach that utilizes PAMmla predictions to computationally engineer SpCas9 variant enzymes in a stepwise manner (Extended Data Fig. 8a).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: ISDE enables prediction of optimal enzymes with greater computational efficiency, enabling rapid sorting by customizable fitness metrics (Extended Data Fig. 8b–d and Supplementary Note 9).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: As a proof of concept, we sought to utilize PAMmla-ISDE to evolve a customized editor to target the human rhodopsin (RHO) P23H mutation causative of retinitis pigmentosa (Ref: 57-59).
context: As a dominant negative mutation, knockout of the mutant allele while leaving the wild-type allele intact can rescue function in heterozygous genotypes (Ref: 60,61). Single-nucleotide mutations can be difficult to target in an allele-specific manner via the sgRNA due to the propensity of SpCas9 to tolerate single-base-pair mismatches between the sgRNA and target DNA (Ref: 56,62). However, if a disease-causing point mutation generates a novel PAM, a high-specificity PAM proof-reading step may be exploited for allele-specific editing (Ref: 56,63,64). Neither wild-type SpCas9 nor SpG are useful, as SpCas9 should efficiently target the wild-type P23 allele and SpG should target both P23 and P23H alleles equally.
outcome: For allele-selective editing of RHOP23H, we utilized PAMmla-ISDE to design enzymes that target an NGTG PAM on the mutant allele and that minimize editing of NGGG on the wild-type allele (Fig. 5a). We initiated PAMmla-ISDE starting with the wild-type SpCas9 sequence and evolved enzymes by sorting for maximized activity on NGTG PAMs while requiring a k < 10−3.7 on NGGG.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: The PAMmla-ISDE fitness plateaued after four cycles of mutagenesis and prediction (Fig. 5b,d and Supplementary Fig. 16a). The optimal enzyme, MRRWMR, was predicted by PAMmla to strongly target NGTG and weakly target NGGG (k = 10−2.1 and 10−3.7, respectively; Fig. 5b). MRRWMR was also the optimal enzyme determined via ISDE using SpG as a starting sequence and different parameters (Supplementary Fig. 16b).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: to further minimize targeting of NGGG
outcome: We also performed a stricter evolution via IDSE... requiring a k < 10−4 on NGGG, which resulted in several enzymes including KRHWMR after four rounds of evolution (Fig. 5c,e and Supplementary Fig. 16c).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: Initial experiments with MRRWMR on three unrelated genomic sites harbouring NGTG PAMs achieved similar editing efficiencies compared with SpG, with minimal editing on a site bearing an NGGG PAM (Extended Data Fig. 9a).

outcome: We then generated a heterozygous RHOP23H HEK 293T cell line... We tested a total of four ISDE-derived enzymes from either campaign in the RHOP23H cell line

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: and observed a preference for editing the mutant over the wild-type allele compared with SpG for all ISDE enzymes (Extended Data Fig. 9b). The MRRWMR enzyme led to the most efficient mutant allele disruption, and KRHWMR resulted in superior allele-specific discrimination with nearly undetectable editing of the wild-type allele in heterozygous and wild-type cells (Fig. 5f and Extended Data Fig. 9b,c). The ISDE-derived MRRWMR and KRHWMR enzymes exhibited approximately 2.5- and 40-fold preferences for mutant P23H over wild-type RHO alleles, respectively (Extended Data Fig. 9e).
outcome: (N/A)

main_content: Use of other recently described Cas9 orthologue nucleases PrCas9 (Ref: 65), CoCas9 (Ref: 66) and GeCas9 (Ref: 66) harbouring PAM preferences that should target the P23H site failed to elicit detectable editing at the RHO locus (Supplementary Fig. 18a–c and Supplementary Note 10).

main_content: to investigate specificity improvements with the allele-selective PAMmla enzymes.
outcome: Next, we performed off-target analyses via GUIDE-seq2

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: On two unrelated genomic loci, we observed enhanced specificity with MRRWMR over SpG and SpRY (Extended Data Fig. 10a–c). When using the RHOP23H sgRNAs in a homozygous P23H cell line, both MRRWMR and KRHWMR also improved on-target specificity by minimizing off-target reads and the number of detected off-target sites compared with SpG and SpRY (Fig. 5g and Extended Data Fig. 10d–f).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: To examine potential structural determinants of the PAM-selective preferences of MRRWMR and KRHWMR
outcome: we modelled their mutations and investigated their contributions to PAMmla-predicted rates on NGTG and NGGG PAMs using SHAP (Fig. 5h–k and Extended Data Fig. 11a–e).

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: For both enzymes, PAM selectivity for a 3rd PAM position T over G largely resulted from the E1219W and R1335M side chains forming a hydrophobic pocket, enabling discrimination via favourable hydrophobic interactions with the T3 methyl group of an NGTG PAM and unfavourable interaction with the polar G3 side chain of NGGG (Fig. 5h,i). SHAP analysis supports that these mutations positively affect recognition of NGTG and negatively affect recognition of NGGG (Fig. 5j,k and Extended Data Fig. 11b–e). Additional common mutations between the two PAMmla enzymes S1136R and T1337R contribute to PAM selectivity and/or potentiation of activity (Extended Data Fig. 11 and Supplementary Note 11).
outcome: (N/A)

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: We assessed the in vivo editing efficiency and allele specificity of MRRWMR and KRHWMR in mouse retinas.
outcome: Subretinal plasmid injections in humanized heterozygous WT-hRHO–GFP/P23H-hRHO–RFP mice at postnatal days 0–2 (P0–P2) were performed before in vivo electroporation.

title: Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning
subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo
main_content: Analysis of on-target editing in transfected cells with MRRWMR revealed mean on-target efficiency of 37% on the P23H allele (up to 59%) and 7.6% on the wild-type allele, leading to 4.8-fold selectivity for editing the mutant allele (Fig. 5l and Extended Data Fig. 10h). Consistent with results in cells, KRHWMR resulted in lower in vivo editing (approximately 20%) but exhibited increased specificity for the P23H over wild-type allele (9.5-fold; Fig. 5l and Extended Data Fig. 10h).
outcome: Together, these findings demonstrate how PAMmla-ISDE can predict novel enzymes for therapeutically relevant edits with no intervening engineering or evolution. PAMmla-nominated enzymes enable cell-based and in vivo allele-selective editing not possible with previously available SpCas9 enzyme variants or Cas9 orthologues.
